title: Graph DFS
description: The other half of graph traversal -- diving deep down one path before backtracking, both recursively and iteratively, plus counting connected components.
objectives: Implement graph DFS recursively and iteratively
Use DFS to count connected components in a graph
Compare when DFS is preferred over BFS

sections.text: 1. DFS on a Graph: Same Idea, New Setting

DFS: Iterative Traversal with a Stack showed that tree DFS dives deep down one branch before backtracking. Graph DFS does exactly the same thing, but -- just like Graph BFS needed a visited set to handle cycles -- graph DFS needs one too.

sections.title: Tracing dfs starting at 0, edges 0-1,0-2,1-3,2-4
steps: dfs(0): mark visited, print 0. Neighbors: 1 (unvisited) -- recurse into dfs(1)
dfs(1): mark visited, print 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 3 (unvisited) -- recurse into dfs(3)
dfs(3): mark visited, print 3. Neighbors: 1 (visited). No unvisited neighbors -- return
Back in dfs(1): no more neighbors -- return
Back in dfs(0): next neighbor 2 (unvisited) -- recurse into dfs(2)
dfs(2): mark visited, print 2. Neighbors: 0 (visited), 4 (unvisited) -- recurse into dfs(4)
dfs(4): mark visited, print 4. Neighbors: 2 (visited). Return -- all calls unwind

sections.text: Compare this order (0 1 3 2 4) to graph BFS's order on a similar graph (0 1 2 3 4, from Graph BFS) -- DFS commits fully to node 1's branch (reaching 3) BEFORE ever looking at node 2, while BFS visits all of 0's direct neighbors first. Same graph, genuinely different exploration strategy.
3. Iterative Graph DFS With an Explicit Stack

Notice the iterative version's output order (0 2 4 1 3) differs from the recursive version's (0 1 3 2 4) -- both are valid DFS traversals, but a Stack's LIFO order naturally processes neighbors in the REVERSE order they were pushed, unlike recursion which processes them in the order encountered. Either is a correct DFS; the exact visiting order among SIBLINGS isn't fixed, only the depth-first STRATEGY is.

The if (visited[current]) continue; check here matters because, unlike graph BFS which marks visited at enqueue time, this pushes neighbors without checking visited status first sometimes leading to duplicate entries on the stack -- the check at pop-time cleans this up safely.

sections.title: How this counts components
points: Component 3: {5}, an isolated node with no edges at all -- still its OWN component
Each time the outer loop finds an unvisited node, it means a NEW component was discovered -- DFS from there marks the entire component as visited in one call

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to loop over ALL nodes (not just from one starting point) when the graph might be disconnected -- a single DFS/BFS call only reaches one component.
Assuming recursive and iterative DFS always visit siblings in the exact same order -- the depth-first STRATEGY is the same, but sibling order can differ based on stack vs recursion mechanics.

Marking visited only at push time for iterative DFS without a pop-time re-check -- this can lead to processing (printing) the same node more than once if it was pushed multiple times before being visited.
Confusing DFS with BFS's use cases -- DFS suits path-existence and component-counting problems well; BFS suits shortest-path problems on unweighted graphs.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does graph DFS relate to tree DFS, and what extra step does it need?
questions.answer: It's the same depth-first strategy -- dive deep down one path before backtracking -- but graphs can have cycles, so DFS needs a visited array/set to avoid revisiting nodes indefinitely, which trees never required.

questions.question: How do you count the number of connected components in a graph using DFS?
questions.answer: Loop over every node; whenever an unvisited node is found, it belongs to a new component -- run DFS from it to mark its entire component as visited, and increment a component counter. The final counter value is the total number of components.

questions.question: Why might recursive and iterative DFS on the same graph produce different visiting orders?
questions.answer: Recursion processes a node's neighbors in the order encountered, while an explicit Stack's LIFO behavior naturally processes them in reverse push order -- both are valid depth-first traversals, but the exact order among sibling nodes isn't fixed by the DFS strategy alone.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Graph DFS extends tree DFS's dive-deep-then-backtrack strategy to graphs, requiring a visited array to handle cycles, exactly as graph BFS required one. It can be written recursively (natural, using the call stack) or iteratively (using an explicit Stack, with a pop-time visited check to avoid reprocessing duplicates). A key application is counting connected components: looping over every node and running DFS from each still-unvisited one, incrementing a counter each time -- since one DFS call fully marks an entire connected component. DFS suits component-counting and path-existence problems, while BFS suits shortest-path problems on unweighted graphs.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Graph DFS = tree DFS with a memory (visited) so cycles don't trap it in a loop. Counting components = walking every unexplored island (DFS from it) and counting islands, one per DFS call from an unvisited start.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What extra step does graph DFS need that tree DFS didn't?
How do you count connected components using DFS?
Why might recursive and iterative DFS visit siblings in different orders?
When would you choose DFS over BFS for a graph problem?

sections.title: Max Area of Island
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n grid of 0's and 1's, return the area (cell count) of the largest connected island (4-directional), or 0 if there is none.

examples.input: grid with several islands, largest has 6 connected land cells

hints: Same as Number of Islands, but instead of just counting islands, DFS returns the cell count of each island and track the max.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: DFS from each unvisited land cell, sinking visited cells to 0 and returning 1 + sum of the 4 recursive calls, tracking the running max area.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) recursion stack worst case

sections.title: Number of Provinces
sections.problemStatement: Given an n x n adjacency matrix isConnected where isConnected[i][j] = 1 means city i and j are directly connected, return the number of provinces (connected components).

examples.explanation: Cities 0 and 1 form one province; city 2 is its own province.

hints: This is just 'count connected components' on a graph given as an adjacency matrix instead of a list.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: For each unvisited city, DFS across the adjacency matrix row marking all connected cities visited, incrementing the province count once per DFS start.

sections.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n height matrix (Pacific touches the top/left edges, Atlantic touches bottom/right edges), water flows from a cell to a neighbor with height <= current. Return all cells from which water can reach BOTH oceans.
hints: Checking forward flow from every cell to both oceans is expensive.

Reverse the problem: DFS/BFS OUTWARD from the ocean border cells, moving to a neighbor only if its height >= current (this simulates water flowing backward, uphill). This marks every cell that CAN reach that ocean.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Run DFS from all Pacific-adjacent border cells (top row, left column) moving to neighbors with height >= current, marking a 'canReachPacific' grid. Do the same from Atlantic-adjacent borders (bottom row, right column) for 'canReachAtlantic'. Cells marked true in both are the answer.

sections.title: Course Schedule
sections.problemStatement: Given numCourses and a list of prerequisite pairs [a, b] meaning you must take b before a, return whether it is possible to finish all courses (i.e. no cycle in the dependency graph).

examples.explanation: 1 needs 0, and 0 needs 1 -- a cycle, impossible to finish.

hints: Finishing all courses is possible if and only if the prerequisite graph has NO cycle.
Detect a cycle via DFS with three states per node: unvisited, visiting (in the current recursion path), visited (fully processed) -- revisiting a 'visiting' node means a cycle.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build an adjacency list (course -> its prerequisites), then DFS each unvisited course tracking a 'visiting' set for the current path. If DFS revisits a node still marked 'visiting', a cycle exists -> return false.

sections.title: Course Schedule II
sections.problemStatement: Same setup as Course Schedule, but return a valid course ORDER to finish all courses, or an empty array if impossible.

examples.explanation: 0 has no prerequisite; 1 and 2 need 0; 3 needs both 1 and 2.

hints: This is topological sort via DFS: after fully exploring a node's dependencies (post-order), push it onto a stack.
Reverse the stack (or use addFirst on a list) to get a valid ordering.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same cycle-detection DFS as Course Schedule I, but add each course to the front of the result list right after it's marked 'done' (post-order) -- this produces a valid topological order automatically.

sections.title: Sort Items by Groups Respecting Dependencies
sections.problemStatement: n items, each optionally belonging to one of m groups (-1 means no group). beforeItems[i] lists items that must come before item i. Return a valid item order respecting both item-level and group-level dependencies, or [] if impossible.
hints: Ungrouped items (-1) each get their own unique group so every item belongs to exactly one group.
Two separate topological sorts are needed: one over ITEMS (via beforeItems edges), and one over GROUPS (a group has an edge to another group if any item in it must come before an item in the other).
Merge results: sort groups topologically, then within each group sort its items topologically.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Assign every ungrouped item its own fresh group id. Build a group-level DAG (edge groupA -> groupB if some item in A is a prerequisite of some item in B) and an item-level DAG (from beforeItems). Topologically sort both (Kahn's BFS via in-degree works well here). If either sort can't include all nodes, a cycle exists -> return []. Finally, walk the group order; for each group, append its items in their own topological order.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(V + E) for items plus O(groups + group edges)
